entries.title: An Economy of AI Agents
entries.subtitle: A survey of how autonomous AI agents may interact with humans and markets in the coming decade
entries.publisher: Anton Korinek/ National Bureau of Economic Research
entries.abstract: This chapter examines emerging developments in artificially intelligent agents capable of planning and executing complex tasks over extended time horizons with minimal human oversight. It surveys key questions for economists regarding agent interactions with humans and other agents, their effects on market structures and organizational behavior, and the institutional frameworks necessary to maintain well-functioning markets.

entries.title: A Note on Human Capital, the Division of Labor, and Artificial Intelligence
entries.subtitle: Argues that AI has a comparative advantage in coordination tasks, which deepens human specialization rather than replacing specialists.
entries.publisher: Peter Isztin / Corvinus University of Economics
entries.abstract: This paper considers the effects of generative artificial intelligence tools on the division of labor. Following Becker and Murphy (1992), the author posits a team with each member assigned to a task. Specialization is limited not only by the extent of the market, but also by coordination costs, which include the need for knowledge in multiple tasks. AI helps the coordination of the team by being a substitute for human generalist knowledge, resulting in a greater number of specialized fields. The introduction of AI also increases the return to knowledge that is equally productive in all tasks. The paper shows that AI agents would typically have a comparative advantage in coordinating specialists, while human agents perform specialist roles.
